next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

The waystation settled after midnight in layers.

First the voices ran out. Then the charger rig Ren had clamped to the Bebop dropped from a hard industrial whine to a low steady hum that lived in the floor. Then the wind took the whole place back. It came along the outer walls in long dry scrapes, lifted dust into the seams around the door, and worried the loose metal skin of the old bay until it ticked and shivered. Dry Well had been built for men who thought a shipment schedule was the same thing as a future. Half the rooms were dead now, sealed with plate and old foam that had gone the color of teeth. The common room still held heat if nobody opened the door too often.

Ren had given them that room with the same economy she'd used for everything else. The cot was yours, the couch was yours, the floor was flat enough if Ed didn't mind it, there were blankets in the locker, the bay light switch stuck halfway and needed a push. Then she'd taken her own bedding, a thin rolled pad and two rough blankets, and carried them back out to her ship without waiting to see whether any of them would object.

Jet didn't.

He ought to have. A young woman turning her home over to a crew she'd spent years tracking across the system should have made him feel something clear and named. Gratitude. Shame. Protective irritation. Instead he felt tired in a way that had weight to it. Tired in the knees, in the base of his skull, in the hand that wasn't metal. Too tired to argue about sleeping arrangements. Too tired to lie to himself about what kind of room this was.

Ed had gone d...

prev_tail_excerpt:
...ered the sock. "Ein says this belongs to the tall sulky one."

"That's me?"

"Today yes."

Ed left as abruptly as Ed had arrived. The door shut. Faye looked down at the sock in Spike's hand because apparently Ed had transferred it at some point during the announcement.

"You gonna wear that," she said, "or frame it."

"Haven't decided."

She laughed, softer this time. It faded without embarrassment.

From the cockpit Jet's voice came over the ship intercom. "Ten minutes. Strap in if you want to keep your teeth."

Faye pushed off the glass. "There's the charm."

Spike stayed one second longer, looking out at the weak square of light ahead. Dry Well was still too far to see with the naked eye, just signal and distance and the knowledge of someone waiting. He thought of Jet at the table, of Faye in the records office hitting yes over and over, of a courier he couldn't picture and an informant he had never known existed while he was alive. None of it arranged itself into anything useful. It just stayed there, weight with no good place to set it down.

He turned and went inside.

Nobody had gone to a room. Jet was at the controls. Faye dropped into the couch and braced a hand against the cushion. Ed had already strapped in upside down somehow, still humming. Ein circled twice under the table and settled. On the main screen, Dry Well resolved at last from a blinking square into a handful of buildings half buried in dust, one bay lit, one ship waiting beside it.

No one said anything brave. No one found a reason to leave. The Bebop lowered its nose toward the basin and kept going.
